result:
## The Unbearable Lightness of Being Amateur

There is a particular, exquisite pleasure in being bad at something. Not willfully negligent, not proudly ignorant, but earnestly, wholeheartedly, beautifully incompetent. I am not speaking of the kind of practiced, performative mediocrity that has become a shtick in our age of viral blooper reels. I mean the genuine, private, slightly breathless state of being a beginner who is no longer young—the kind of person who, past the age where aptitude is expected, picks up a fiddle, or a language, or a set of watercolours, and produces not art, but evidence of effort. This is the realm of the Amateur, in the word’s oldest, most sacred sense: one who loves.

We live, of course, in the Cult of the Expert. Our world is siloed into domains of hyper-specialized knowledge, each with its own gatekeepers, jargon, and metrics of success. To have a hobby is often merely the first step in a covert mission to monetize it. The casual guitarist dreams of a Spotify presence; the weekend baker eyes a pop-up shop; the diarist scribbles with a half-glance toward a Substack newsletter. The act is corrupted at its source by the phantom audience, by the potential for conversion into capital or clout. The pure, unobserved doing—the doing for its own sake, for the silent, stubborn love of the thing—feels almost subversive, a quiet act of resistance against the relentless engine of utility.

My own cathedral of glorious failure is the piano. I arrived late at its altar. My childhood was not filled with the disciplined torture of scales; no stern teacher metronomed my afternoons into submission. I came to it in my thirties, a time when the brain’s plasticity is said to be hardening like cement, when fingers are meant to be proficient at typing emails, not tracing arpeggios. I bought a second-hand digital piano that fit in my small apartment. It was a serious-looking thing, weighted keys mimicking the heft of the real, but it was also silent to the outside world, a concession to neighbours and to my own shame. I could be terrible in total privacy.

And I was. I am. My relationship with the instrument is one of profound misunderstanding. Sheet music is a cryptic map of a country I cannot fathom. My eyes and my hands are in a constant, low-grade civil war. The left hand, tasked with the humble, foundational work of chords, develops a mutinous mind of its own, lagging a fraction of a second behind the right, or pressing too hard, turning a gentle accompaniment into a jarring thud. The melody, under my care, stumbles. It loses its rhythm, its phrasing, its soul. What should be a flowing river of notes is a series of puddles, connected by hesitant, clumsy steps.

For months, my repertoire consisted of the first eight measures of Beethoven’s “Für Elise.” I could navigate that famous, looping opening phrase with a sort of cautious determination. But the piece, as pieces do, eventually demands change. It introduces a new motif, a shift in harmony. My world would crash. I’d hit a wrong note—a flat, ugly sound in the context of the sweet minor key—and my entire system would freeze. The spell of the eight-measure illusion was broken. I’d start again from the beginning, a Sisyphus in a suburban living room, forever rolling the same sonic boulder up the same hill, only to watch it tumble back down at the exact same spot.

A rational person would quit. An ambitious person would hire a teacher, drill exercises, “hack” the skill. But I am an Amateur. So, I persisted in my glorious ineptitude. And something peculiar began to happen in those hours of repetition and failure. I stopped trying to *play* the piano and started trying to *listen* to it. When my left hand fumbled a chord, I heard, truly heard, the dissonance it created. I learned the texture of a minor seventh versus a major. I felt, in my bones, the longing of a suspended chord waiting to resolve. My mistakes were not just errors; they were experiments in sound. I was deconstructing music through accident.

The silence of the digital piano became a blessing. Freed from the judgement of an audience, even an imagined one, the process became the entire point. There was no recital to prepare for, no exam to pass, no follower count to impress. There was only the physical reality of the moment: the cool feel of the plastic keys, the slight resistance then yield of the mechanism, the sound blooming in my headphones, a private universe contained between my ears. In that space, time changed. The anxious, forward-leaning time of deadlines and schedules—what the Greeks called *chronos*—dissolved. In its place emerged *kairos*: the opportune moment, the eternal now. An hour would vanish in the pursuit of a single, clean transition between two chords. It was a form of meditation, albeit a cacophonous one.

This, I think, is the secret power of the Amateur: the reclamation of time as something to be spent, not saved. We are obsessed with time-management, with life-hacks, with optimizing every minute for maximum yield. Our leisure itself becomes a project to be managed. But the Amateur wastes time magnificently. She pours hours into a garden that will only produce a few crooked tomatoes. He spends a weekend building a model ship that will forever sit slightly askew on a shelf. I butcher Beethoven for an audience of one, and in the butchering, discover a note combination so strangely beautiful it would never appear in the score. This “waste” is not waste at all; it is an offering on the altar of pure engagement. It is time made sacred through unproductive love.

The Amateur also rediscovers the physical world. In an age of abstraction—of screens, of virtual meetings, of digital assets—the Amateur’s pursuit is irreducibly material. It is about the grit of charcoal on rough paper, the smell of turpentine, the satisfying *thwack* of a tennis ball hitting the sweet spot of a racket, the frustrating knot in a skein of yarn, the tactile feedback of clay spinning on a wheel. My piano is a digital intermediary, but the act is physical. My shoulders ache from tension. My fingertips grow calloused. I am *in* my body, negotiating a complex, spatial puzzle: here is the key, here is the symbol on the page that represents it, here is the muscle memory that must bridge that gap. This embodied learning is humbling. The mind, so proud and verbal, is rendered a bumbling novice. The body, with its slow, stubborn intelligence, must lead.

There is a democracy in amateurism, too. The expert’s world is hierarchical. There are tenured positions, bestseller lists, gallery representations, championship titles. The Amateur’s world is flat and open. A retired plumber and a university professor can stand side-by-side at the community woodworking bench, equally baffled by a dovetail joint. Their shared struggle is the great leveler. Conversation flows not from credentials, but from shared frustration and the occasional, hard-won breakthrough. “How did you get that stain so even?” “What did you do when the glue seeped out here?” This is the language of collaboration, not competition. It builds a different kind of community, one based on mutual aid in the face of a beautiful, difficult thing.

Of course, this idyll has its shadows. The love of the Amateur can curdle into the despair of the Dabbler. There is a fine line between persistent enjoyment and stubborn masochism. The initial romance of a new pursuit inevitably gives way to the long, arid plateau of intermediate skill. This is where most hobbies are abandoned—in the valley of “good enough to see how bad you are, but not good enough to feel the joy of fluency.” This plateau is the true testing ground for the Amateur spirit. Does the love of the *act itself* provide enough sustenance to continue, even when progress is imperceptible? For the expert-in-training, the plateau is a problem to be solved with better technique, more hours, a new coach. For the Amateur, the plateau might just be the destination. The struggle *is* the point. The endless return to the same challenging passage is not a failure; it is the ritual.

I think of the Japanese concept of *wabi-sabi*: the beauty of imperfection, impermanence, and incompleteness. A cracked bowl, repaired with gold lacquer (*kintsugi*), is more treasured for its flaw. The Amateur’s output is all *wabi-sabi*. The slightly off-pitch note in a sung ballad carries more emotional truth than studio-perfect autotune. The wobble in a hand-thrown pot gives it character no machine-made vessel can possess. Our culture of polish and precision—from Instagram filters to AI-generated art—seeks to erase these fingerprints of humanity. The Amateur proudly leaves her fingerprints all over the work. They are the signature of presence.

My piano playing will never soothe a crowd. It will never be recorded, never be performed. Its entire purpose is the vibration it sends through the floorboards, the concentration it etches on my face in the dim evening light, the tiny spark of connection when my hands, for once, do what I ask and a phrase sings out clear and true. That spark is not the anticipation of future mastery. It is a complete joy in itself, momentary and self-consuming.

In a society suffering a crisis of meaning, where grand narratives have collapsed and we are left with the exhausting project of self-optimization, the Amateur offers a different path. It is not the path of achievement, but of absorption. It is the radical decision to do something for no reason other than that it is difficult, and beautiful, and *there*. It is a vote for depth over breadth in a world skimming the surface. It is a commitment to a tiny, personal sovereignty, a patch of ground where you are both the ignorant monarch and the devoted subject.

The word “amateur” has been used as an insult for so long we have forgotten its etymology. *Amare*. To love. The professional is motivated by reward, the expert by mastery, the genius by a force they often cannot name. But the Amateur is motivated by love alone. It is the most fragile and the most durable of fuels. Fragile, because it offers no external validation; it must be perpetually stoked from within. Durable, because it can survive endless failure, neglect, and frustration. Love does not demand success. It only demands engagement.

So, I will return to my silent piano tonight. I will likely stumble over the same passage that defeated me yesterday. I will create more accidental dissonance than harmony. But for an hour, I will be gloriously, purposefully useless. I will be out of time, deep in the material world, engaged in a dialogue with centuries of genius that I can hear but cannot replicate. And in that dialogue—in that loving, failed attempt—I will be entirely, unshakably myself. Not a better version, not a more productive version. Just a human, in a room, making sounds for the sheer, unbearable lightness of being an amateur.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
